title: Chicken and Andouille Gumbo
subtitle: Louisiana gumbo built on a dark roux with chicken, sausage, and the holy trinity
description: Gumbo is the definitive dish of Louisiana — a thick, intensely flavoured stew built on a dark roux that is cooked until the colour of dark chocolate, then combined with the holy trinity, chicken, andouille sausage, and okra. The dark roux is what makes gumbo fundamentally different from any other stew: it is a flavour agent, not a simple thickener, and requires twenty to thirty minutes of constant stirring over a medium heat. The patience required is the entire point.
sourceNotes: Gumbo is Louisiana's state dish, with roots in West African, Native American, and French Creole culinary traditions. The dark roux method is consistent with Louisiana cookery from the eighteenth century. The Picayune's Creole Cook Book (1901) contains several gumbo recipes; Paul Prudhomme's Louisiana Kitchen (1984) is the modern canonical reference.

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based is essential for the roux — thin pans scorch.
For the constant stirring during the roux — do not use a whisk, which cannot reach the corners of a casserole effectively.

glossaryTerms.definition: Flour cooked in fat over a medium heat for 20–30 minutes until it reaches the colour of dark chocolate. Unlike a French blond roux used for thickening, a Cajun dark roux adds intense nutty, almost bitter flavour — it contributes colour and depth to gumbo but less thickening power than a lighter roux.
The aromatic base of Cajun and Creole cooking: onion, celery, and green bell pepper.
Ground dried sassafras leaves — a traditional Choctaw seasoning used to thicken and flavour gumbo. Added off the heat at the end of cooking or served at the table.

ingredients.notes: Or lard — not butter, which burns
Sliced into 1 cm rounds — or smoked sausage
Sliced into 1 cm rounds — or frozen

steps: In a large, heavy casserole over a medium heat, combine the oil and flour. Stir constantly with a wooden spoon for 20–30 minutes until the roux reaches a very dark chocolate colour. This requires patience and constant stirring — the roux will look pale for the first 10 minutes and then colour quickly toward the end. Do not walk away. If it smells burnt, the heat is too high; reduce it.

The moment the roux is dark enough, add the onion, celery, and green pepper all at once — the vegetables will sizzle dramatically and stop the roux from continuing to darken. Stir well and cook for 5–6 minutes until the vegetables soften.
Add the garlic, smoked paprika, thyme, cayenne, salt, and pepper. Cook for 1 minute.
Gradually ladle in the hot chicken stock, stirring constantly after each addition until smooth before adding the next ladle.
Add the chicken pieces, sausage slices, okra, and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer.
Cook uncovered for 45–55 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the chicken is cooked through and falling from the bone and the gumbo has thickened.
Remove the chicken pieces. Pull the meat from the bones and return the meat to the gumbo, discarding the bones and skin. Remove the bay leaves. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Serve in bowls over cooked white rice, scattered with spring onions and parsley.

content.text: is the defining step of gumbo and cannot be rushed or abandoned mid-cook. Commit twenty to thirty minutes of constant stirring before you start anything else — the whole dish depends on the roux being properly dark. Use a heavy pan, medium heat, and do not leave the hob.

Combine {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and {{plain-flour}} of flour in a large heavy casserole over a medium heat. Stir constantly with a wooden spoon for 20–30 minutes until dark chocolate coloured. The moment it is done, add the
(onion, celery, and green pepper) all at once. Stir and cook for 5–6 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and spices for 1 minute.

Ladle in {{chicken-stock}} of hot stock gradually, stirring after each addition. Add the chicken, {{andouille-sausage}} of sausage, {{okra}} of okra, and bay leaves. Simmer uncovered for 45–55 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the gumbo has thickened. Remove the chicken, pull the meat from the bones, and return the meat to the pot. Remove the bay leaves, taste, and adjust seasoning. Serve over white rice with spring onions and parsley.
Traditional Cajun gumbo is sometimes finished with

— ground dried sassafras leaves — stirred in off the heat just before serving. It adds a subtle herbal, slightly bitter flavour and thickens the gumbo further. It is not essential but is the traditional finishing touch. Add 1 tsp off the heat if you have it.
